speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number nine. I'm talking to Mrs. Magda Klein. It is July 8, 1996. Mrs. Klein, you were telling me about how you managed to get food stamps from this person who wanted to deny it to you because you hadn't been thoroughly cleansed. What did you find after you got back to your home town of Mandok? Was your family there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to get a certificate from the Jewish organization which was at the shul. They call it Tobacco Temple, Dohany Temple, in Hungary. And there were this office where we got the certificate. Meantime, there were mothers with beautiful pictures of their daughter, did you see it, did you see it? I said, sweetheart, I don't know it. I don't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back to our town. We found that all the windows of our house was taken off. We found that the makeshift police station was in one of our stores. And no furniture, a few pieces of straw on the wood. We went in, and we were so heartbroken. We were exhausted. We lay down on the-- on the plain, wooden floor. And we were laying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next morning when we got up, we found, because we had double doors, between the two doors that there were a bucket of bean soup. Who put it there, I don't know, left the container there, everything. Then we started to look around and decided to go to some of the people who came back already, who were in the working battalion, and they came back earlier, who were collecting all the valuables from the leftover Jews, whatever they could put their hand on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went over one of the women. I said to her, look, we had a lot of beautiful things. You must have some of the shirts or some of the things that was collected here, because I see that your closet is filled up. And I have not a shirt. I have nothing. I'm still with the same haftling clothes, what I got in Auschwitz. So she said, well, if you insist upon it, look at my closet. And by that time, I got so disgusted. I didn't recognize any, and I didn't take any. I said, if she is happy, let her have it. And I mean, I was just, by that time, too tired. I mean, it was just too much. And we didn't find anybody. I was asking some of the other people who came back, did you hear anything? Did you see anything? Then about half a year later, there was this man who used to be a tailor. He says to me that I was together with your father and your brother, and they died just before liberation in Bergen-Belsen. I said to him, thank you for telling me. And it was very, very hard to pick up the pieces. Did you go up into the attic and look for any of the jewelry you had taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did go up to the attic, and I found the jewelry. It was a bracelet, and it was two rings. And it was-- I don't remember, but it was about six pieces. At that time, I thought that what a big value, you know, what a big thing. So we were trying to borrow different garden tools to start to cultivate the garden. And we were trying to look around.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we were working in the garden, it wasn't even 12 o'clock, but we could hear our mother's voice, the babe, and we were children. And the mother used to call, children, come for lunch. So then I looked at my sister. I said to her, am I crazy? I said, did you hear our mother calling? She said yes. I mean, it was something. And we were like shipwrecked. We-- we just didn't know where to pick ourself up. How long did you stay in Mandok?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there '47. In '47, I-- I wrote to my aunt that I would like-- we would like to come to United States. So my uncle wrote back, do not get married, because I'm working with a Congressman, and I want to have the same names when I'm sending you the affidavit. Being that it was the experience with my younger brother with his affidavit. His name had to be on the affidavit. So my uncle said do not get married. But unfortunately, when you went through so much suffering and all that, you are trying to finagle. You try to do something. You can't just sit in one place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had heard that there is a place in Budapest that they are taking in orphaned children like we were. So I said to my sister Elizabeth, I take you up there-- which is another story-- on top of the wagon. And the Russians are pushing the packages off, which I didn't have, but I was afraid that they push me off. So we went up to Budapest. I placed her in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had this woman whose husband I knew, I had asked her if she would take her in as an apprentice to teach her how to make undergarment. She was nice enough. She did it. Then my older sister and my cousin was there then. She fell in love, my cousin fell in love, with a young man who went to Germany because he felt that from Germany, he would able to emigrate to United States, because he had relatives here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said that once he goes there, he would see about the situation. He will write for my cousin to go after. So when he wrote that my cousin should go after him, I wanted her to go, because she was so much in love with him. So I took the jewelry, and I put it in a Crisco can with fat in it. And I took it over to one of the-- the smith men, and he put the letter over it. So I told my older sister, I said Clara, go with her to Germany. This way she won't be alone, because such a [INAUDIBLE] that-- that it was impossible. So I went up to Budapest. I made the arrangement for money, that there was this man who knew the-- the conduct, the motor man. And he was taking the two girls, put it into the [INAUDIBLE], where they're feeding them water, and he was hiding them. And they were going to the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to Germany. She married the fiance. And my sister didn't find her place. I mean, she missed us very much. I missed her very much. And I found that I must have been very stupid that I let her go. And after that, I got a-- a paper that mean that we were registered before the war to come to this country, that maybe I'd be able to renew and get some of [? the ?] me-- that their names was on it. So I came to this country in '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come over, did you fly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my cousin sent me ticket to come, which cost $1,200, which laid around. For my $25 a week payment, I paid him back. And I feel very good about it, that I did. My aunt, at that time, was crying. And she said, how could he take the money from you?

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, Aunt [? Rose, ?] don't worry. I wanted to give it to him. He didn't ask me for it. So she said, but you have any money left over? If you get sick, what are you going to do? I said, Aunt Rose, I'm not going to get sick. I have $5 left. And I was flying. I was so happy that I don't owe him anything. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married-- I was here in this country in '51-- '47, June 12, and my fiance came after me in '51. And it just happened that that blonde girl whom I went to to steal potatoes, she was the one who said, I have four brothers, three of them is married, but one is not. And if we go home, I would like you to be my sister-in-law. And while I was in Hungary, we met, and we fell in love. But I didn't want to stay in Hungary. I said if he is [INAUDIBLE], he will come after me, and we will get married. And it did happen that way. So it is like a fairy tale. I suffered four years without belonging to anybody, without getting attention from anybody. Because most my cousins, they were born in this country. They didn't speak Hungarian, except one cousin of mine. And it was really very, very hard, very hard. Because whenever I would have a chance to talk about my suffering or my situation, my uncle wanted to know. His wife didn't want to know. And this was going on in quite a few different occasions, where they just couldn't lend me an ear to listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then it got to the point that I wasn't anxious to talk because of nothing to talk about. It's a new life. It's a new thing. So who is interested about somebody's story from way back from Europe? With all that you went through, and you went through so much, it's been over 50 years. Do you have a message you would like to leave for your two sons, for your three grandchildren, something you'd like to say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I hope they will never, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] never go through the same thing what I did. They will able to live life like any normal human being. That just because they are Jewish, and they didn't commit any crime, that doesn't mean that they should be persecuted, they should be belittled. They should be treated like any human being. And if and ever, which I hope it will never, comes a time where they have to save their life, they should give every penny what they have, and they should try to escape from the situation as fast and as soon as they can. They do not wait until it's too late. Because my father always used to say, oh, nothing will happen with us. The Russians are right here at the border. They going to come in any-- any day, any minute, and they will. Unfortunately, he was wrong. The Germans had time to put us to ghetto for six weeks. They had time to put us on the wagon and take to Germany, to Auschwitz. And unfortunately, the whole world closed their eyes. They were bombing in Nuremberg. They were bombing England day and night. But they never came to bomb the tracks to Auschwitz. They never, never-- everything was forgotten. The whole world closed their eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have to protect yourself. If you have a chance, give everything what you have to save your life. Because life is the most important thing. You cannot replace it. Thank you, Mrs. Klein. Thank you so much for sharing your experiences with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? If I would know-- ?] That picture what I'm looking from here, that's my father, Ferenc Czigler. And he was born in [? Tougier, ?] 1896. And he was my father. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old do you think he was on that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I no idea. When they took us away in '44, he was 48 years old. But he doesn't look like a 48 years old. I think the most he might be is like 30 to 35. That's the way I look at it. This picture is my mother with my older brother. And far as I remember her telling us, she made that outfit for my brother. She was excellent with her hand. And she sent this picture to her sisters and brothers here to this country, United States. That's how I managed to have this picture today to show how my mother looked. That picture represents my brother, the youngest brother, Charlie. And I don't think he was more than two, two and a half years old, if he was that old. And also I received these pictures from my aunt. Otherwise, I wouldn't have it. This certificate is the original certificate that we received after liberation in [INAUDIBLE] from the burgermeister. And that was the certificate that entitled us for the traveling in the cattle car, or on top of the cattle car, to go towards Hungary. This certificate is the original certificate that, after liberation, we arrived to Budapest, and we got that at the Tobacco Shul, the Dohany Temple in Hungaria. And this entitled us to get 1,500-- not 1,500, 150 forint that we were able to buy something for ourself. And we were entitled to travel on the train to get back to our town where we were born. The picture that you are looking at is-- was taken a few months before I came to United States in '47. And the one to the left as I'm facing it is my former myself in those days. And when I look at it today, I don't want to believe it, but that once upon a time, I looked like that. And the next lady next to me is my present sister-in-law, Margaret. And that time, she was waiting for her husband to come back, but unfortunately, it never happened. So today she is my beloved sister-in-law. The picture what you are looking at is the three sisters together. Towards my left, in the pinkish dress, is my older sister, Clara. And in the middle is myself. And to the right is my younger sister who looks older than any one of us. But she was born in 1930, and this picture must have been taken in '75, '76 in this country. And unfortunately, the years, the suffering, that she went through as a young child, it left a very big mark. The picture that is showing here is my older sister, Clara, with her granddaughter, Jamie. We had one of the family get togethers, and she came. So that's her. The picture that is front of me is my beloved aunt. She is 97 years old. She is the only sister my mother had left from my mother's side. And there she is in an adult home in Catskill, in a beautiful, beautiful home. I hope she will be there many years. There's the picture of my husband and myself. We were visiting my cousin in Cape Cod after I had the first loss of my hair. And that's us with my own hair. The picture that you are looking at is my two beautiful daughter, my sons' wife. And the two handsome guys are our son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right is our older son Steven with his wife Renee. And to the left is our younger son Thomas with his wife [? Terry. ?] The picture that you are looking at is our three beautiful grandchildren. The-- to the left as we are facing it is our older granddaughter, Heather. She was 10 years old at-- last December 25. And the young man to the right is Robert. He was nine years old in February. And the young lady in the middle is Emily, who was seven years this June. And these are our precious, valuable treasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Klein, would you like to tell me who is sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This young, good looking man is my husband Alex, Alex Klein. And we've been married for the last 44 years. Thank God I could say we are happily married. And I hope I could say it 20 years from now, that we are married. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Klein, is there anything you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am seconding the motion. I'm very happy that I find a girl like my wife and being married to her for all these years. And thank God we are very, very happily married. And hopefully we will last, not as much as we had in it, but we will last a long time in the best of health. That's the only thing I wish for both of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still thank your sister for bringing you together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. I do. Without her, probably we would never get together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually we could thank her because she got to know me through stealing the potatoes, and also get to know me, and she liked me. And I'm very happy she did, because I like her very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually they say that marriages are made in heaven. Our marriage is made in hell, but thank God it turned out to be close to heaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: To be heaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the-- that's the fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. You're an inspiration to everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are more than welcome. And we thank you, Ellen, and we thank Steven for being so nice and patient with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been a pleasure.
